content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441 322-6444

October 18, 1982


K. D. Lyders
Oxnard City Attorney
305 W. Third Street
Oxnard, CA 93030

Re: Your Request for Advice
    Our No. A-82-158

Dear Mr. Lyders:

     Thank you for your letter requesting our advice as to possible conflicts of interest arising from certain redevelopment decisions pending before the Oxnard Redevelopment Agency. The facts, as stated by you, are as follows:

FACTS

     The Oxnard City Council also sits as the Redevelopment Agency Board. The City has been undergoing redevelopment for some time, with project areas in several phases. Two Agency board members (Councilman Takasugi and Councilman Lopez) own parcels in or near the projects. You have enclosed a map (copy attached as Exhibit "A") which shows the locations of the relevant projects and parcels.

     The approximately eight block R-108 redevelopment project in the upper left (of Exhibit "A") has been substantially completed with the construction of the "A" Street mall which extends for three blocks between Third and Sixth Streets. The only remaining parcel is that indicated for the mall tower which the Agency is in the process of selling to a developer. The disposition process involves numerous decisions by the Agency which is involved in the financing and leasing of an integral parking structure. The mall tower will constitute a 14-story office building leased to various tenants with parking provided by the City.

K. D. Lyders
October 18, 1982
Page 2


     The Agency also has a Central City Revitalization
Project (CCRP) which covers a large area, but is being
implemented in smaller segments shown as areas A, B, and C
on the map.  Each segment is for all practical purposes a
separate project.

     Councilman Takasugi owns a market at the corner of
Seventh Street and Oxnard Blvd. which he formerly operated,
but now leases to others for operation as a Japanese
specialty foods grocery.  The market has its own parking
area.

     Councilman Lopez owns three parcels:

     L-1 is a small commercial office building at the
corner of Third and C Streets about one block from the
boundary of the R-108 project.  It has some profes-
sional offices and a furniture store among its tenants.

     L-2 is a 50' x 140' vacant lot at the corner of
Sixth and C Streets across the street from the CCRP and
R-108 boundaries.

     L-3 is a parcel within the boundaries of area C in
the CCRP.  It has a few old residential dwellings on it
which will be replaced once the property is fully
developed.

     There are numerous small parcels comparable in size to
those owned by these councilmen both within and in the
vicinity of these redevelopment projects.  This entire area
is the old downtown section of Oxnard containing many
rundown buildings, vacant lots, and other evidence of
blight.  Area A is proposed for redevelopment as a trans-
portation center serving rail, bus and local transit
passengers.  However, this is mostly for the benefit of
long distance travelers and is not expected to increase
local commuting into downtown Oxnard.  Area C is proposed
for residential and commercial uses.  The uses for Area B
have not been designated and they are not under active
consideration at the present time.

     You have previously advised the councilmembers to
refrain from participating in decisions involving project
areas within which their parcels are located.

K. D. Lyders
October 18, 1982
Page 3


QUESTIONS

The questions which these two councilmen desire to have
answered are as follows:

     1.  Can Councilmen Lopez and Takasugi participate in
decisions involving the mall tower in R-108?

     2.  Can Councilman Lopez participate in decisions
involving Area A, the transportation center?  Although
Areas A and C are part of the CCRP, it should be assumed
that they are two separate and distinct projects.

     3.  Can Councilman Takasugi participate in decisions
involving Areas A and C?  Again, assume that Areas A, B and
C are separate and distinct projects.

ANALYSIS

Councilman Takasugi

     Financial Interest.  We will assume from the Facts that
Councilman Takasugi has an interest in real property worth
more than $1,000 (Government Code Section 87103(b)/).
In addition, his lessee undoubtedly pays him at least $250
annually in rent and constitute a source of income (Section
87103(c)).  Therefore, our analysis must turn to the issues
of whether or not this financial interest will be materially affected by the decisions at hand and, if we conclude
in the affirmative, whether the effect on his interest will
be distinguishable from the effect on the public generally.

     Material Financial Effect (Mall Tower Decisions).
Councilman Takasugi's market is approximately four blocks
(over 1,000 feet "as-the-crow-flies") from the mall tower.
Apparently, the only agency decision which would likely
affect surrounding businesses of the type which his
property involves are the decisions regarding the parking
structure.  Any effects upon the rental value (he rents the
market out), or the fair market value of Councilman
Takasugi's property or on the lessee thereof appear to be
remote and speculative.  Therefore, we conclude that there

_________________
     1/  All statutory references are to the Government
Code unless otherwise specified.  The Political Reform Act
("Act") is found at Government Code Sections 81000-91014.

K. D. Lyders
October 18, 1982
Page 4


is no reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on
his financial interests.2/ Consequently, we need not now
address the "public generally" issue.

Material Financial Effect (Project Areas A and C):

Project Area A is virtually as far away from Councilman
Takasugi's property as is the mall tower. The decisions on
the transportation center are somewhat analogous to the
parking facility in the mall tower. With his market
located at such a distance, it is unlikely that persons
arriving at the transportation center would then visit his
market. The addition of the transportation center to the
greater downtown area will undoubtedly improve that area
and have some enhancement of fair market and rental
values. However, the effects on Councilman Takasugi's
property or his lessee's businesses are likely to be
insignificant and, hence, not material.3/

Project Area C, on the other hand, is immediately
adjacent to Councilman Takasugi's property and will consist
of residential, as well as commercial redevelopment. This
would seem to be quite analogous to the facts in our
Gillmor Opinion, supra. There, the Commission held that
the effect on Mayor Gillmor's commercial enterprises would
be material. Consequently, many of the decisions relating
to Project Area C will foreseeably have a material financial effect upon his financial interest and he should
disqualify himself as to these. We will be happy to
respond to any questions on specific decisions.

Public Generally (Project C). It seems clear that the
effects of decisions having a material effect on Councilman
Takasugi's property, utilized as a market, will differ from
the effects on the general public in Oxnard.4/ So will

_______________________

2/ See generally, Oglesby Opinion, 1 FPPC Opinions
71, No. 75-083. Owen Opinion, 2 FPPC Opinions 77, No.
76-005; Gillmor Opinion, 3 FPPC Opinions 38, No. 76-089;
Thorner Opinion, 1 FPPC Opinions 198, No. 75-089; and Brown
Opinion, 4 FPPC Opinions 19, No. 77-024, fn.3 at 4 FPPC 21
(copies enclosed).

3/ 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702(a).

4/ See, Owen Opinion, supra; Gillmor Opinion, supra;
and Brown Opinion, supra.

K. D. Lyders
October 18, 1982
Page 5


the effects on his lessee's business as a market. There-
fore, we conclude that these effects will be distinguish-
able from the effects on the public generally.

Councilman Lopez

     Financial Interest. Again, we assume from the facts
the presence of the requisite financial interest in real
property in excess of $1,000 in each of the three
properties. We also assume that Councilman Lopez' tenants
at his L-1 property each constitute a source of income to
him of $250 or more during the last 12 months. We turn
again to the issues of materiality and the public
generally.

     Materiality (Mall Tower). Councilman Lopez' L-1
property is located approximately two blocks from the mall
tower. You have indicated that some parking problems may
arise as a spin-off from the development of the mall tower
office building. You have indicated that the office space
created there will not be in direct competition with
Councilman Lopez' building for tenants. It is uncertain
what impact, if any, its presence will have on the tenants'
businesses. Consequently, we would conclude that a
material financial effect is reasonably foreseeable only if
construction of the mall tower will affect Councilman
Lopez' property value at L-1 by the requisite amount set
forth in 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702(b)(2) (copy
enclosed). That is an evaluation more readily made by you
and him at the local level.

     Councilman Lopez' L-2 property is approximately three
blocks from the mall tower and is presently vacant.
Therefore, the same test would apply as for property L-1
above. If decisions involved in the mall tower project
will affect the L-2 property sufficiently to meet the
standards of the regulation, then materiality would exist.

     Councilman Lopez' L-3 property is geographically much
more remote and projected for a possibly different use. We
would conclude that a material financial effect on this
property is not reasonably foreseeable.

     Public Generally (Mall Tower). Under the reasoning in
the Owen, Oglesby and Gillmor Opinions, supra, we conclude
that the effect (if found to be material) on the L-1 and
L-2 properties would be distinguishable from the effect on

K. D. Lyders
October 18, 1982
Page 6


the public generally. There are not enough additional
surrounding lot owners who would incur a similar effect to
constitute a significant segment of the public generally (2
Cal. Adm. Code Section 18203).

Materiality (Project Area A). You have indicated that
the proposed Transportation Center will principally involve
a new inter-city bus terminal (Greyhound) and new railroad
facilities for inter-city service to Los Angeles and other
communities. A local bus transfer site will be provided to
intermesh with these facilities. Currently, local bus
services are available to these properties. We see no
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on L-1,
L-2, L-3 or the L-1 tenants from decisions involving
Project Area A.

CONCLUSION

Based upon the facts provided to us, as stated in this
letter, Councilman Takasugi may participate in decisions
involving the mall tower and Project A; but not Project C
decisions if they will have a material financial effect.
Councilman Lopez may or may not be able to participate in
decisions involving the mall tower, but may participate in
decisions involving Project A.

Should you have questions regarding this letter, I may
be reached at 916/322-5901.

Sincerely,

Robert E. Leidigh
Counsel
Legal Division

State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission

P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • •    Administration • •    Executive/Legal • •    Enforcement • •    Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322.5662            322.5660              322.5991              322.6441              322.6444

September 23, 1982


K. D. Lyders
City Attorney
305 W. Third St.
Oxnard, CA 93030

Re: A-82-156

Dear Mr. Lyders

Your letter requesting advice under the Political
Reform Act has been referred to Robert E. Leieigh, an
attorney in the Legal Division of the Fair Political
Practices Commission. If you have any questions about your
advice request, you may contact this attorney directly at
(916) 322-5901.

We try to answer all advice requests promptly.
Therefore, unless your request poses particularly complex
legal questions, or unless more information is needed to
answer your request, you should expect a response within 21
working days.

Very truly yours,

Barbara A. Milman
Chief
Legal Division

BAM:plh
Enclosure

CITY OF OXNARD
CALIFORNIA

OFFICE OF THE
CITY ATTORNEY
305 W. THIRD STREET
OXNARD, CALIF. 93030
(805) 488-4311 EXT. 270

K.D. LYDERS
City Attorney
MARK G. SELLERS
Deputy City Attorney

September 15, 1982

Fair Political Practices Commission
Legal Division
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Re: Conflict of Interest Opinion Request

Gentlemen:

I am requesting your opinion regarding the existence of possible
conflicts of interest involving participation of two members of
the Redevelopment Agency (which is the City Council) in certain
Agency decisions. The potential conflict arises by reason of
real property owned by these members in and near redevelopment
project areas.

Enclosed is a map to help you understand the problem. The approximately eight block R-108 redevelopment project in the upper left
has been substantially completed with the construction of the
"A" Street mall which extends for three blocks between Third and
Sixth Streets. The only remaining parcel is that indicated for
the mall tower which the Agency is in the process of selling to
a developer. The disposition process involves numerous decisions
by the Agency which is involved in the financing and leasing of
an integral parking structure.

The Agency also has a Central City Revitalization Project (CCRP)
which covers a large area but is being implemented in smaller
segments shown as areas A, B, and C on the map. Each segment is
for all practical purposes a separate project.

Councilman Takasugi owns a market at the corner of Seventh Street
and Oxnard Boulevard which he formerly operated but now leases
to others for operation.

Fair Political Practices Commission                    September 15, 1982
                                                                Page 2


Councilman Lopez owns three parcels:

     L-1 is a small commercial office building at the
corner of Third and C Streets about one block from
the boundary of the R-108 project.

     L-2 is a 50 X 140 vacant lot at the corner of
Sixth and C Streets across the street from the
CCRP and R-108 boundaries.

     L-3 is a parcel within the boundaries of area
C in the CCRP.

There are numerous small parcels comparable in size to those owned
by these councilmen both within and in the vicinity of these
redevelopment projects. This entire area is an old downtown section
of Oxnard containing many rundown buildings, vacant lots, and other
evidence of blight. Area A is proposed for redevelopment as a
transportation center serving rail, bus, and local transit passengers.
Area C is proposed for residential and commercial uses. The uses
for Area B have not been designated and it is not under active
consideration at the present time.

Before areas A and C were actively being considered in the rede-
velopment process, I advised both Councilmen that they were
not disqualified from participating in decisions involving the
R-108 project completion. The theory was that each of them owned
small, individual parcels not significantly different from hundreds
of similar parcels in the area. Neither of them had participated
in the initial decisions approving the project. The final comple-
tion of the project thus did not appear to involve decisions which
would have a material financial effect on their properties or if
there would have been such an effect, it would not be distinguish-
able from the effect on the hundreds of similar parcels in the
vicinity. I have at all times advised them to refrain from partici-
pating in decisions involving the areas within which their parcels
are actually located.

The questions which these two councilmen desire to have answered
are as follows:

1. Can Councilmen Lopez and Takasugi participate in decisions
involving the mall tower in R-108?

2. Can Councilman Lopez participate in decisions involving
Area A, the transportation center? Although areas A and C

Fair Political Practices Commission                September 15, 1982
                                                            Page 3


are part of the CCRP, it should be assumed that they are two
separate and distinct projects.

3.  Can Councilman Takasugi participate in decisions involving
areas A and C?  Again, assume that areas A, B, and C are
separate and distinct projects.

Please let me know if you need further information on these
matters.  Your opinion will be very much appreciated.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        K. Darlene Dunklee
                                        City Attorney

PORTION OF THE CITY OF OXNARD

CITY OF

Oxnard

CITY ATTORNEY • 300 W. THIRD STREET • OXNARD, CALIFORNIA 93030 • (805) 486-4311 EXT. 2270

K. DUANE LYDERS, CITY ATTORNEY

March 22, 1984


Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Attention: Mr. Robert D. Blasier, Jr.
Counsel, Enforcement Division

Re: Your March 15, 1984, Letter Concerning Mayor Takasugi

Dear Mr. Blasier:

I have been asked by Mayor Takasugi to respond to your letter dated March
15, 1984, since he is preparing to leave on an extended trip. In that
letter you state that you have "decided not to bring formal charges"
against Mayor Takasugi based on his participation in a council decision on
March 15, 1983, requesting the Planning Commission to consider rezoning of
certain property in a redevelopment area referred to as Area C. You refer
to your letter to me dated October 18, 1982, in which you reviewed the
ability of then Councilman Takasugi to participate in decisions involving
the Mall Tower and Area A as well as Area C, concluding that he could
participate in decisions involving the Mall Tower and Area A, "but not
project C decisions if they will have a material financial effect."

I regard your assumption that Mayor Takasugi has violated the Political
Reform Act as wholly unwarranted and regret that you did not discuss the
matter with the Mayor or me before you sent your letter. The Mayor's
participation in the decision was pursuant to my advice which, in my
opinion, was entirely consistent with the analysis in your letter of
October 18, 1982.

Your October 18, 1982, letter refers to the fact that the Mall Tower is
over 1,000 feet from the Takasugi property and states that

"any effects upon the rental value . . . or the fair
market value . . . or on the lessee thereof appear to
be remote and speculative. Therefore, we conclude
that there is no reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on his financial interests."

Mr. Robert D. Blasier, Jr.
March 22, 1984
Page 2


You further state that since the Transportation Center (Area A)" is
virtually as far away from Councilman Takasugi's property as is the Mall
Tower," the effects on his property are "likely to be insignificant, hence,
not material."

Your analysis with respect to Area C, on the other hand, observes that the
area is "immediately adjacent" to the Takasugi property and that the
situation is analogous to the facts of your Gillmor opinion wherein Mayor
Gillmor held multiple financial interests which you concluded could
foreseeably be affected by a project directly across by a street. Your
analysis as to Area C thus concluded that

     "many of the decisions relating to Project Area C
     will foreseeably have a material financial effect
     upon his [Takasugi's] financial interest and he
     should disqualify himself as to these."

I believe that the March 15, 1983, decision is not one requiring
disqualification. This decision did not involve the entire Area C but
rather only the single block bounded by C and B Streets and Seventh and
Eighth Streets. This block is almost as far from the Takasugi property as
is Area A and its development would have no more effect on the Takasugi
property than development of the Mall Tower or Transportation Center. I
feel that your conclusion in the October 18, 1982, letter that the effects
of these projects on the Takasugi property are "remote and speculative" and
"insignificant" are sensible and correct and the same conclusion applies to
the project in the corner of Area C most remote from the Takasugi
property.

I trust that the foregoing explanation will dispose of the matter. If you
have any further questions or comments I would request that you discuss
them with me before you publish any written conclusions.

Very truly yours,

K. W. Lyders
City Attorney

fd

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 · SACRAMENTO, 95804 · · · 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance · · Administration · · Executive/Legal · · Enforcement · · Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662          322-5660          322-5901          322-6441          322-6444

March 15, 1984

Honorable Nao Takasugi
Mayor of Oxnard
300 W. 3rd
Oxnard, CA

RE: FPPC No. GC-82/438

Dear Mayor Takasugi:

On September 15, 1982, Oxnard City Attorney K. D. Lygers
requested advice from this agency on your behalf relating to
several redevelopment projects in Oxnard. One of those
redevelopment areas is known as "Area C" and located across the
street from a parcel of real property and business owned by you
(your parcel is located at 660 South Oxnard Boulevard).

On October 18, 1982, our staff counsel Robert Leigh
responded in writing to the City Attorney's request for advice.
Our advice with respect to Project C was that you should not
participate in decisions involving Project C if they would have a
material financial effect on any financial interest held by you.
You were also requested to contact us if you had any questions. A
copy of our advice letter is attached for your information.

It was brought to our attention that on March 15, 1983, you
voted for and signed a resolution recommending that the planning
commission hold public hearings to consider rezoning Project
Area C so that the "central city revitalization project" could
proceed. Being considered for Area C was a mixed office and
residential development with a high rise office tower as the focal
point. You at no time consulted with us concerning the propriety
of your participating in this governmental decision relating to
Project Area C. It is our view that had you done so, we most
likely would have advised you to not participate.

We have now been advised that the developer who intended to
develop Area C has filed for bankruptcy and the planning process
for Area C must now start over again. Because of this factor, we
have decided not to bring formal charges against you for your
participation in the governmental decision of March 15, 1983.

Mayor Takasugi
March 15, 1964
Page Two


I strongly suggest that in the future you contact us prior
to any consideration of future plans with respect to Area C.
Participation by you in the future in such decisions without
seeking advice will be considered in determining the appropriate
enforcement remedies to be pursued.

Should you have any questions concerning any of the above,
you should contact me within five days upon receipt of this
letter.

Very truly yours,

Robert D. Blasiez, Jr.
Counsel
Enforcement Division

Attachment
RDBisf

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 9581.
Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Enforcement
(916) 322-5662        322-5660            322-5901        322-6441

June 20, 1984


Mr. K. D. Lyders
City Attorney
City of Oxnard
300 W. Third Street
Oxnard, CA 93030

RE: Mayor Takasugi
     FPPC No. GC-82/438

Dear Mr. Lyders:

     Thank you for your letter of March 22, 1984 concerning our prior enforcement inquiry into possible violations of the Political Reform Act by Mayor Takasugi. Because of your letter, I have again reviewed our file in this matter.

     It is accurate that the specific vote cited in our letter concerned the city block bounded by "C" and "B" Streets and Seventh and Eighth Streets. However the memorandum dated March 1, 1983 from Robert Prescott and submitted to the council make it clear that the rezoning in question was the first step in the development of both blocks 142 and 144, an area consisting of almost one-half of the entire area C. Further, the resolution signed by the Mayor recommending the public hearing for parcel 142, specifically refers to the "Priority Actions Plans and Programs for the Central City Revitalization Project." That document deals with development plans for all of area C.

     Based on the above, it is still our opinion that the decision in question is such an integral part of area C development plan that the Mayor would have been well advised to contact the Commission prior to his participation.

Mr. Lyders
June 20, 1984
Page 2


As I'm sure you are aware, by contacting us in advance and
following our advice, the Mayor is entitled to certain legal
immunities from prosecution in the future. We trust that the
Mayor will avail himself of this service in the future.

Very truly yours,

[signature]

Robert D. Blasier, Jr.
Legal Counsel
Enforcement Division

sections.conclusion:
Based upon the facts provided to us, as stated in this 
letter, Councilman Takasugi may participate in decisions 
involving the mall tower and project A; but not project C 
decisions if they will have a material financial effect. 
Councilman Lopez mayor may not be able to participate in 
decisions involving the mall tower, but may participate in 
decisions involving Project A.

sections.analysis:
Councilman Takasugi 
Financial Interest. 
We will assume from the Facts that 
Councilman Takasugi has an interest in real property worth 
more than $1,000 (Government Code Section 87l03(b)17}. 
In addition, his lessee undoubtedly pays him at least $250 
annually in rent and constitute a source of income (Section 
87l03(c)}. 
Therefore, our analysis must turn to the issues 
of whether or not this financial interest will be materi-
ally affected by the decisions at hand and, if we conclude 
in the affirmative, whether the effect on his interest will 
be distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. 
Material Financial Effect (Mall Tower Decisions). 
Councilman Takasugi's market is approximately four blocks 
(over 1,000 feet "as-the-crow-flies") from the mall -tower. 
Apparently, the only agency decision which would likely 
affect surrounding businesses of the type which his 
property involves are the decisions regarding the parking I 
structure. 
Any effects upon the rental value (he rents the 
market out), or the fair market value of Councilman 
Takasugi's property or on the lessee thereof appear to be 
remote and speculative. 
Therefore, we conclude that there 
1/ All statutory references are to the Government 
Code unless otherwise specified. 
The Political Reform Act 
("Act") is found at Government Code Sections 81000-91014. 


K. D. Lyders 
October 18, 1982  
is no reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on 
his financial interests.~/ Consequently, we need not now 
address the "public generally" issue. 
Material Financial Effect (Project Areas A and C): 
Project Area A is virtually as far away from Councilman 
Takasugi's property as is the mall tower. 
The decisions on 
the transportation center are somewhat analogous to the 
parking facility in the mall tower. 
With his market 
located at such a distance, it is unlikely that persons 
arriving at the transportation center would then visit his 
market. 
The addition of the transportation center to the 
greater downtown area will undoubtedly improve that area 
and have some enhancement of fair market and rental 
values. 
However, the effects on Councilman Takasugi's 
property or his lessee's businesses are likely to be 
insignificant and, hence, not material.1/ 
Project Area C, on the other hand, is immediately 
adjacent to Councilman Takasugi's property and will consist 
of residential, as well as commercial redevelopment. 
This 
would seem to be quite analogous to the facts in our 
Gillmor Opinion, supra. 
There, the Commission held that 
the effect on Mayor Gillmor's commercial enterprises would 
be material. 
Consequently, many of the decisions relating 
to Project Area C will foreseeably have a material finan-
cial effect upon his financial interest and he should 
disqualify himself as to these. 
We will be happy to 
respond to any questions on specific decisions. 
Public Generally (Project C). 
It seems clear that the 
effects of decisions having a material effect on Councilman 
Takasugi's property, utilized as a market, will differ from 
the effects on the general public in Oxnard. 4/ 
So will 
~/ See generally, Oglesby Opinion, 1 FPPC Opinions 
71, No. 75-083. 
Owen Opinion, 2 FPPC Opinions 77, No. 
76-005; Gillmor Opinion, 3 FPPC Opinions 38, No. 76-089; 
I 
Thorner Opinion, 1 FPPC Opinions 198, No. 75-089; and Brown 
Opinion, 4 FPPC Opinions 19, No. 77-024, fn.3 at 4 FPPC 21 
(copies enclosed) • 
1/ 
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702(a) • 
i/ See, Owen Opinion, supra; Gillmor Opinion, supra; 
and Brown Opinion, supra. 


K. D. Lyders 
October 18, 1982  
the effects on his lessee's business as a market. 
There-
fore, we conclude that these effects will be distinguish-
able from the effects on the public generally. 
Councilman Lopez 
Financial Interest. 
Again, we assume from the facts 
the presence of the requisite financial interest in real 
property in excess of $1,000 in each of the three 
properties. 
We also assume that Councilman Lopez' tenants 
at his L-l property each constitute a source of income to 
him of $250 or more during the last 12 months. 
We turn 
again to the issues of materiality and the public 
generally. 
Materiality (Mall Tower). 
Councilman Lopez' L-l 
property is located approximately two blocks from the mall 
tower. 
You have indicated that some parking problems may 
arise as a spin-off from the development of the mall tower 
office building. 
You have indicated that the office space 
created there will not be in direct competition with 
Councilman Lopez' building for tenants. 
It is uncertain 
what impact, if any, its presence will have on the tenants' 
businesses. 
Consequently, we would conclude that a 
material financial effect is reasonably foreseeable only if 
construction of the mall tower will affect Councilman 
Lopez' property value at L-l by the requisite amount set 
forth in 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702(b) (2) 
(copy 
enclosed). 
That is an evaluation more readily made by you 
and him at the local level. 
Councilman Lopez' L-2 property is approximately three 
blocks from the mall tower and is presently vacant. 
Therefore, the same test would apply as for property L-l 
above. 
If decisions involved in the mall tower project 
will affect the L-2 property sufficiently to meet the 
standards of the regulation, then materiality would exist. 
Councilman Lopez' L-3 property is geographically much! 
more remote and projected for a possibly different use. 
We 
would conclude that a material financial effect on this 
property is not reasonably foreseeable. 
Public Generally (Mall Tower). 
Under the reasoning in 
the Owen, Oglesby and Gillmor Opinions, supra, we conclude 
that the effect (if found to be material) on the L-l and 
L-2 properties would be distinguishable from the effect on 


K. D. Lyders 
October 18, 1982  
the public generally. 
There are not enough additonal 
surrounding lot owners who would incur a similar effect to 
constitute a significant segment of the public generally (2 
Cal. Adm. Code Section 18203). 
Materiality (Project Area A). 
You have indicated that 
the proposed Transportation Center will principally involve 
a new inter-city bus terminal (Greyhound) and new railroad 
facilities for inter-city service to Los Angeles and other 
communities. 
A local bus transfer site will be provided to 
intermesh with these facilities. 
Currently, local bus 
services are available to these properties. 
We see no 
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on L-l, 
L-2, L-3 or the L-l tenants from decisions involving 
Project Area A.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: The questions which these two councilmen desire to have 
answered are as follows: 
1. 
Can Councilmen Lopez and Takasugi participate in 
decisions involving the mall tower in R-l08? 
2. 
Can Councilman Lopez participate in decisions 
involving Area A, the transportation center? 
Although 
Areas A and C are part of the CCRP, it should be assumed 
that they are two separate and distinct projects. 
3. 
Can Councilman Takasugi participate in decisions 
involving Areas A and C? 
Again, assume that Areas A, Band 
C are separate and distinct projects.

CONCLUSION: Based upon the facts provided to us, as stated in this 
letter, Councilman Takasugi may participate in decisions 
involving the mall tower and project A; but not project C 
decisions if they will have a material financial effect. 
Councilman Lopez mayor may not be able to participate in 
decisions involving the mall tower, but may participate in 
decisions involving Project A.
embedding.first_500_words: State of California Fair Political Practices Commission P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest (916) 322-5662 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441 322-6444 October 18, 1982 K. D. Lyders Oxnard City Attorney 305 W. Third Street Oxnard, CA 93030 Re: Your Request for Advice Our No. A-82-158 Dear Mr. Lyders: Thank you for your letter requesting our advice as to possible conflicts of interest arising from certain redevelopment decisions pending before the Oxnard Redevelopment Agency. The facts, as stated by you, are as follows: FACTS The Oxnard City Council also sits as the Redevelopment Agency Board. The City has been undergoing redevelopment for some time, with project areas in several phases. Two Agency board members (Councilman Takasugi and Councilman Lopez) own parcels in or near the projects. You have enclosed a map (copy attached as Exhibit "A") which shows the locations of the relevant projects and parcels. The approximately eight block R-108 redevelopment project in the upper left (of Exhibit "A") has been substantially completed with the construction of the "A" Street mall which extends for three blocks between Third and Sixth Streets. The only remaining parcel is that indicated for the mall tower which the Agency is in the process of selling to a developer. The disposition process involves numerous decisions by the Agency which is involved in the financing and leasing of an integral parking structure. The mall tower will constitute a 14-story office building leased to various tenants with parking provided by the City. K. D. Lyders October 18, 1982 Page 2 The Agency also has a Central City Revitalization Project (CCRP) which covers a large area, but is being implemented in smaller segments shown as areas A, B, and C on the map. Each segment is for all practical purposes a separate project. Councilman Takasugi owns a market at the corner of Seventh Street and Oxnard Blvd. which he formerly operated, but now leases to others for operation as a Japanese specialty foods grocery. The market has its own parking area. Councilman Lopez owns three parcels: L-1 is a small commercial office building at the corner of Third and C Streets about one block from the boundary of the R-108 project. It has some profes- sional offices and a furniture store among its tenants. L-2 is a 50' x 140' vacant lot at the corner of Sixth and C Streets across the street from the CCRP and R-108 boundaries. L-3 is a parcel within the boundaries of area C in the CCRP. It has a few old residential dwellings on it which will be replaced once the property is fully developed. There are numerous small parcels comparable in size to those owned by these councilmen both within and in the vicinity of these redevelopment projects. This entire area is the old downtown section of Oxnard containing many rundown buildings, vacant lots, and other evidence
